comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I type this, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. This week's classic of Western occultism is A Vision by William Butler Yeats. As most people know (I hope!), Yeats was one of the 20th century's great poets, but he was also a lifelong occultist and a high-ranking member of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn. A Vision was the result of a joint magical working he and his wife Georgianna (also a Golden Dawn adept) carried out over many years. I know of nothing with which to compare it other than the I Ching; like that Chinese classic, it's a set of symbols expressing a cyclic theory of time, but A Vision works with 28 phases, each of which is linked with a symbolic phase of the Moon, and each of which expresses a particular moment in the life of an action, a creative project, a human life, a series of incarnations, or an age of the world. Deep?  Plunge into it and it just keeps going. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

[IMAGE:1]
And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

1) Today I started the First Degree AODA work, and have noticed Belteinne is next week. I'm interested in celebrating it, but having read through the Druid Grove Handbook see two problems with celebrating it using the rituals there: first, there is no separate Belteinne ritual, and I don't want to start trying to craft one for a while, since at this point I don't have enough experience to even begin to start to figure out how to do that.

I'm willing to put time in to practice it,but I don't know where to look to find a good ritual. Do you know a simple ritual for Belteinne I could do with basically no background?

2) I have a lot of free time right now. As an absolute beginner I don't want to push myself with spiritual exercises, but I want to put it to use for developing my spirituality. I'm assuming studying the books I'll need to know later for the AODA second degree would be a wise use of my time, and working through the Dolmen Arch (which I've selected as my Druid exploration), but are there other things I can do?

3) I'm planning to start doing astrology by hand, but can't find a table of logarithms for calculating planetary motion. Do you know where I could get such a table? Ideally I'm looking for something in print, but something available online would also work.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The older Druid Revival tradition doesn't celebrate cross-quarter days like Belteinne -- just the solstices and the equinoxes. Gerald Gardner rescued the cross-quarter days from almost total neglect in the late 1940s when he was working on the first draft of Wicca, and they went into some branches of Druidry from that source. AODA doesn't celebrate them. So you can put some time into AODA ritual methods before the summer solstice.

2) As an absolute beginner you run a real risk of overdoing, resulting in burnout. You might consider using the time to study herbalism, ecology, Celtic legends, or some other field that relates to Druidry but won't risk straining your undeveloped metaphysical muscles.

3) Most books of house tables have logarithms in the back, and so do old-fashioned ephemerides such as the Rosicrucian Ephemeris. You might also consider learning how to use a slide rule, which works on the logarithmic principle.

comments.content:
1. I’m the commenter from last week who asked about a passage from Robert Wang’s The Qabalistic Tarot. There’s another passage from that book that’s been causing me to scratch my head lately. This is where he discusses how the Minor Arcana of the Tarot also have astrological attributions in the form of a planet acting in a sign:

‘There are, in fact, a number of Minor Arcana cards where the meaning of Planets in Signs differs from that commonly accepted, because the card combines Sephirothic and astrological symbolism. Here it must be kept in mind that the Zodiac is the Mundane Chakra of Chokmah; it appears in the lowest of the Four Worlds. The cards represent the totality of that to which the Signs of the Zodiac refer.’

Any idea what he means here?

2. If each Sephirah is active in all Four Worlds, what is the justification for certain Worlds being more closely associated with certain Sephiroth than others? For example, Briah is often held to be closely associated with Chokmah and Binah, but all Sephiroth have an archangelic level, so shouldn’t all Sephiroth be equally active in Briah?

3. Let’s say I get Two of Wands in a Tarot divination. Twos are usually attributed to Chokmah, and Wands to fire and the mental level of being. This would imply that the Two of Wands represents the influence of Chokmah in the mental realm. But Chokmah is not part of the Ruach and very remote from daily experience – how can it possibly have any influence, and what shape does this influence take?

4. The Sphere of Yesod is often attributed to the etheric body, which makes sense given its connection to the Moon. However, it’s also commonly attributed to the unconscious mind. I don’t understand how this second attribution is possible given that it’s located at the bottom of the Astral Triangle, the level of concrete consciousness. My understanding is that the personal unconscious corresponds to the Ethical triangle, or the Higher Self, composed of Chesed / Geburah / Tiphareth. The higher you go up the Tree of Life, the more you you ascend into the unconscious, first the personal unconscious and then the collective unconscious.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Have you meditated on it?

2) What's going on here is that two competing ways of thinking about the Four Worlds have been mushed together. There's one in which there's a Tree of Life in each world, and another in which a single Tree spans all four worlds. I suggest that you simply note the discrepancy, and see which version works for you in practice.

3) This is one of the reasons why I don't use that set of attributions of Tarot to the Tree of Life in my own practice. No, it doesn't make much sense -- nor does it make sense to map the Trumps onto the Tree starting from the top, when the Trumps are so clearly a process of spiritual ascent beginning with ordinary (Foolish) consciousness.

4) Here again, you're trying to conflate different models that don't necessarily fit together well. If you want to use a psychological model, Yesod is the preconscious or subconscious mind, the "machine language" on top of which the operating system of consciousness functions. The higher levels are not subconscious but superconscious.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
3) But in doing so, aren't you severing the connection between the Tarot and the Tree of Life? I thought it was established in the modern Qabalah that they're supposed to be interlocking systems of symbolism, and whatever is expressed in one can be expressed in the other. I don't think I've seen a single resource on the Hermetic Qabalah that doesn't assign the Minor Arcana to the Sephiroth in the usual way.

4) That makes a lot of sense, actually. So in this psychological model, Yesod corresponds to something like the Freudian id. In that case, do dreams occur in the sphere of Yesod or are they a glimpse of the superconscious levels above it?

I suggest picking up Willam Gray's The Talking Tree and anything in the French tradition, just for starters.

Since you mention the I Ching, I figure this is a good opportunity to get your opinion on a divination I did recently with the Book of Changes. My question was "How am I doing in my occult studies so far?" and I got hexagram 63 (After Completion) changing to hexagram 37 (The Family) with line 6 moving. My interpretation is as follows:

In the I Ching, hexagram 63 indicates a situation that is past the point of no return. The great river has already been crossed. The trigrams are water above and fire below, antagonistic elements that move in opposite directions, bringing to mind the image of a cauldron of water boiling over a fire. This indicates that a force has been unleashed that must now be controlled. The important thing is not to look back, especially considering the text of line 6.

Hexagram 37 relates to the family structure in ancient China, where the man was active outside the household and the woman inside the household. I see this as a metaphor for the psyche as understood in occult philosophy, with the husband representing the Higher Self and the woman the Lower Self. In the judgment text, it is the perseverance of the woman (i.e. the personality in incarnation) towards the goals of the Higher Self that is essential. Alternatively, the second hexagram could also be a more literal reference to the home, as in "Stay home. This is where you will make the most progress."

Does this seem reasonable?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, but I'm not at all an expert in I Ching divination, so if you're uncertain of your interpretation you probably need to run it past someone who knows the I Ching a good deal better than I do.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, if you have just hung a cauldron full of water over the fire, would it not be best to allow the experienced housekeeping member of the family tend to it minutely and constantly instead of expecting a boiling cauldron to tend to itself while you go out into the world and tend the fields and the livestock? One is put in mind of those amusing tales where the peasant men and women exchange jobs for a day. Who better than the soul within the house can be sure what's cooking away in the hot pot stays at a perfect simmer? Left untended, it could boil over, put out the fire and spoil badly, even turn poisonous with bacteria proliferating in wet warmth instead of killed off by proper heating. Or come out half-cooked and disgusting. Or else boil too hot, get way overdone, and be served up tasteless and unpalatable. Attention to these details of soul-keeping make a big difference in how much healthful nutrition you get out of the pot.

In other words, be circumspect, not open, closely tend to matters within, keep up with what you started, don't get above yourself and start expecting public applause and glittering medals just for doing your own hearthkeeping and homework.

comments.title: Your Translation of Picatrix
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I just finished your translation (along with Christopher Warnock’s) translation of the Picatrix and I have a question.
First, let me say that I found the work extremely interesting and a great insight into the culture and time period (the tenth century Islamic world). However, I also found it rather disturbing. Not because of the animal sacrifice, though I’m sure that would disturb a lot of modern practitioners. What I found disturbing is what I perceive as very simplistic (almost Harry Potteresque) representation in many of the recipes. To put it bluntly, either the original author was extraordinarily gullible, or he was using s lot of fake information to hide the real esoteric materials he was trying to impart. I’m inclined to think the latter for two reasons. First, all of the prospective authors have good reputations for scholarship, meaning that they were unlikely to be extraordinarily gullible; and two, I don’t think a document lacking real esoteric wisdom (some of which I could see without too much effort) would have survived more than a thousand years and been incorporated into so many subsequent works.

But I wanted to get your opinion on it. I think that many of the recipes requiring strange, exotic, and often toxic materials are intended to lead the uninitiated astray (which I think you guys suggested in the introduction) and to hide the real meanings behind the text, as well as the portions that hold the accurate materials. What do you think? Am I warm? Because if, in fact, those recipes were intended to be taken seriously, it must have really sucked to be a chicken in a town full of perspective wizards in tenth and eleventh century Al Andalus, especially if you were a rooster with a divided comb.

Oh, and I used your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature Generator, so I will sign off under my nom de mage,

-- Puce Transcendent Puppy (AKA, Lenn)

replies.title: Re: Your Translation of Picatrix
replies.content:
It's important not to insert modern ways of thinking about the world into texts like Picatrix. We tend to think of "esoteric meanings" as referring entirely to the mind and spirit, and material references as wholly exoteric and probably "blinds.' They didn't think that way. Are you familiar with the doctrine of signatures? If not, the short version is that the material form of a thing -- for example, the divided comb on a rooster -- was a reflection of the spiritual energies that brought that thing into being.

So a lot of the recipes in Picatrix do seem to be intended to be used as given. They are, in important ways, comparable to hoodoo workings (which of course also involve a lot of animal parts and the like). The higher dimension of the work, which is of course woven into the text in ways that make it hard to tease out, can be found in the rituals of spiritual and planetary initiation given in the text -- and of course there are things in there that are deliberate blinds, and in fact deathtraps for the foolish.

replies.title: Re: Your Translation of Picatrix
replies.content: Thanks, JMG.  This puts it more into perspective for me.

replies.title: Re: Your Translation of Picatrix
replies.content: Just to chime in as well: It should be kept in mind that the Picatrix in its Castilian Spanish then Latin translations is not overly faithful to Maslama al-Qurtubi's (d. 964) Arabic original, the Goal of the Sage (Ghayat al-hakim) -- it's rather shorter, with many deletions and additions, and the more difficult theoretical sections are often garbled (a very common feature of Arabic-to-Latin translations). Some of the grossest operations are among those bits added, so reflect not tenth-century Andalusian Islamic culture but rather the thirteenth-century Latin Christian culture of the translators. In other words, the Latin version is a somewhat darker and less philosophically sophisticated book than the Arabic original, which continued to be popular as a manual of natural philosophy down to the colonial period. It's definitely less Harry Potteresque! ;)

replies.title: Re: Your Translation of Picatrix
replies.content: Of course! Since I know Latin rather than Arabic, though, I translated the text I had to hand -- and of course it was some consolation that I was working with the same text that inspired Marsilio Ficino, Giordano Bruno, Simon Forman, and Elias Ashmole, among many others.

replies.title: Re: Your Translation of Picatrix
replies.content:
Oh, needless to say! Just thought I'd clarify that it's not as representative of tenth-century Andalusian Islamic culture as the OP assumed, understandably, since the Arabic original has yet to be translated into English or even properly edited, bizarrely but predictably enough. But yes: it's a testament to the power of the text that it was still able to inspire those European greats even in truncated and garbled form! Books are living beings after all, whatever their descent...

Speaking of which, I'm sure there are more recent grimoires for which the same principle holds -- its core shines through despite all the vicissitudes of transmission. Any you'd put in the same category?

comments.title: Agriculture as the Fall again?
comments.content:
I have been haphazardly meditating on how and whether the local First Nation (a Coastal Salish group) cosmology fits into the Qabalistic system, as Fortune says Crowley called the Qabbalah a perfect file system, and one can better understand how anything works (better) in the cosmos if you can determine where it fits on the Tree.

I got sidetracked in Fortune's polarity discussions in the CosDoc - to start, I’ve been thinking about the gender of the earth, moon and sun and other natural forces. Something universal about what these bodies represent in terms of Force and Form, Active and Passive looks compelling using Egyptian, Greek and Christian cosmology, as well as bits of the other European myth structures I was familiar with (which isn’t saying much, granted), which usually had the Moon and Earth as a Feminine force, and the Sun as Masculine. The Coast Salish, however, did not seem to originally gender/anthropomorphize the Earth much, but one anthropological account I found had it as male, and the Moon and Sun are consistently male and female, respectively. Happypandatao has also said on the Other Blog that Hindu female deities are active and male are passive, so it’s not even necessarily universal to proto-indo-european root cosmology to assign the Force/Active and Form/Passive gender split the same way… I think that I have a good hypothesis for this discrepancy that fits based on the law of the planes from the Cosmic Doctrine and Qabbalistic system, and wanted to run it by you –

Humans that developed agriculture live by the Sun cycle. Thus, the sun-force to agriculturalists is Active to their lives, and, as they are people who markedly reshape the earth, their livestock, and crops to meet their needs, they conceive of themselves as relatively Active on a relatively Passive landscape (taken to high extremes with our culture’s concept as The active force and everything else entirely Inert). So they identify the Sun, as the body that governs/sparks their activities, with Force, male principle. The nature forces of Life, that are receptive to that Force in the life-circuit in the worldview of the agriculturalist are thus feminine – the Moon, the Earth. (As indeed the Earth eventually gets conceived as passive and receptive to us.)

Humans that practiced primarily hunting and gathering, with limited intensive practices that were more about tweaking a natural process than directing it (clam gardens, controlled burning, pruning and thinning food forests, rice aquaculture) were governed by the Lunar cycles (e.g. the 13 Moons of the WSANEC harvest cycle), and what the Earth is already doing – thus Earth and Moon are the source of the Force that humans are subject to. Since the Sun-Moon/Earth relationship is still known as a force circuit, therefore, the Sun becomes Female… I guess. Representative of the heavens, the Form that contains the world? I haven’t seen anything in their stories to suggest one way or the other if the Sun as actually receptive to the Moon actually makes sense, or if they would just have it because of the intrinsic need to recognize the polarity. However, another explanation presents itself by looking back to the Tree, where we see a universal applicability in the heavenly body that serves as a symbol of Tiphareth in the Sun…

Tiphareth is the highest concept of god humans can perceive directly, and it’s a human-shaped concept, because it’s shaped to fit our minds. For a society that views itself as Passive to the natural forces in their life, they might make the Tiphareth astrological body Passive/Female as well, as the highest human-shaped godform perceivable – and, crucially, receptive then, in a circuit not with the Earth and Moon, but to the still higher forms of godhead that we can’t perceive directly.

If I’m onto something about the way that humans get their food determining the way they perceive the polarity of natural forces, and thus intuiting the existence of higher planes of the cosmos more easily… then it does give some credence to Daniel Quinn’s theory that the invention of agriculture was the Fall. Earning our keep by the sweat of our brow, and needing to have scads of kids to work the fields - greatly multiplying Eve’s pain in childbirth, as it were – was not an arbitrary punishment, it was the Thing itself -what naturally followed from the Knowledge. Something that was a natural outgrowth of our development as humans, but that would inevitably cut off our connection to higher planes of the cosmos through our understanding reflected through ourselves. Something that, then, we would have to work our way back to as part of our evolution, because at some point we’d have to merge our idea of ourselves as materially like god as active principle in the cosmos, at the nadir of involution. (The Hindus, of course, have a very advanced spiritual system, and old agricultural practice, so perhaps had already gone further than other human cultures on working out that forces switch polarities further up the planes, heading back up the path of evolution ahead).

I like my little theory, but I still balk at it, because it still looks too much like it buys into the hunter-gatherers are more ‘primitive’ than agriculturalists mindset, or, conversely, the noble savage because agriculturalists are thus Fallen. I suppose it all just comes back to the fact that it’s not really that we tried to control nature as a binary between Good Savages who didn’t and Evil people who did, but rather that all human cultures have had to find the balance of how much control, and when to be active and when passive relative to the forces we work with. I suppose the hunter gatherers who made their giant mammal prey go extinct after the ice age could also be people who Fell by cranking their entitlement to nature up to 11… for that matter, all of the Turtle Island myths about ‘that time humans got arrogant or lazy and didn’t hold their end of the compact with the gods and had a flood/drought/something that made them realise the error of their ways’ would fall in their. So each culture could have started back the evolutionary track regardless of specifically agriculture…

Uh… so the question is… am I onto something, or er, just engaging in mental onanism…?

replies.content:

comments.title: Plato and "God"
comments.content: I’m reading a dialog of Plato, Phaedo, translated by Hugh Tredennick, and I’m stuck on a simple question: On the second page, there is the line “I could not help feeling that even on his [the soul of Socrates] way to the other world he would be under the providence of God…”; note that “God” is with a capital G. Now God was strictly a Jewish deity at that time, so why is he being referred to when we know the ancient Greeks had their pantheon with Zeus as the supreme deity? Did the translator simply translate “Zeus” as “God”, or is he referring to Hermes who escorted newly departed souls to the realm of the afterlife, or is something else afoot? More generally, can you recommend a companion or guide to Plato?

replies.title: Re: Plato and "God"
replies.content:
In ancient Greek literature it's common to see the term theos, "god," used as a generic label for divine presence. Honest translations into English turn that into "a god" or "the god," but there's been a lot of stealth Christianization of the ancient Greeks, and this is a good example. The actual text reads θείας μοίρας, "a divine summons," the whole passage is translated here as "I thought that in going to the other world he could not be without a divine call..." So the Jewish deity -- whom nobody in Plato's time called by the English word "God," by the way! -- was never involved.

As for a good companion or guide to Plato, depends on what you're looking for. There's a huge literature on the subject, and it's been long enough since I dipped into it that I'm not sure what to recommend. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Plato and "God"
replies.content:
Well, this is just odd. My partner is currently reading the Phaedo and asked me exactly this question the other night, phrased very similarly. We're also reading Gregory Shaw's book on Iamblichus together right now (see a few questions back). He often asks questions here, always anonymously. I assumed these both were his questions.

I just talked to him and said, "hey, did you see JMG answered your questions?" and he looked at me blankly and said "I didn't ask any questions this week." I'm not assigning anything too meaningful to this coincidence, but it makes me feel warm and happy to know that there are strangers out there following the same trails we are. Tracks in space!

comments.title: Postive thoughts
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

About 2 years ago you gave another reader some excellent advice regarding the self-reinforcing nature of having a negative outlook on life (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/29585.html?thread=1680273#cmt1680273). This topic has been a struggle for me as well, for my whole adult life.

On the one hand I feel that having a pessimistic outlook takes the sting out of bad things happening, since I have anticipated them already and on the other I feel that having an optimistic outlook is only possible when disregarding the state of the world, possibly bordering on being ignorant.

I'm familiar with the concept of "ternary thinking" but I'm unable to see the way of combining these two extremes of thinking into a third way that connects both ideas.
I was wondering if you had any advice on how to get past the trap of binary thinking around a positive life outlook?

replies.title: Re: Postive thoughts
replies.content:
"If I keep a green bough in y heart, the songbird will surely come." Chinese proverb.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Postive thoughts
replies.content:
Sure. Every day, choose one situation of the kind you tend to brood about, and write out the worst realistic outcome you can think of -- one or two paragraphs is enough. Then write out the best realistic outcome you can think of -- and make it realistic, not pie in the sky. Then write out a third outcome that falls halfway between the two.

Keep doing this day after day. At intervals, glance back over what you've written and compare your written outcomes to what actually happened. Notice where what actually happened tends to fall on the worst<-->middle<-->best spectrum.

comments.title: Kids meditation
comments.content:
I have an 8 year old son who is very much the opposite of me at that age. I was a nerdy bookish kid who learnt to read so early I can't remember not reading, who did math in his head and followed all the rules etc. My son is pretty much the opposite - a gregarious "jock" who is good at every sport he plays, very charming and great insight into people, but a late reader, behind on math in school etc (possibly with a touch of low self-esteem about his academic achievements). He is intelligent - some times he says things with the most astonishing insight - especially about people.

His key issues appear to be lack of self-control (especially emotional regulation) and lack of focus (almost the same thing really). Those lead to everything else.

He's basically the poster child for a "fixed mindset" child instead of "growth mindset" - he's the kind of kid who will tell you how amazing he is at [X] and then try do X and fail and give up immediately calling himself "stupid" and melting down without seriously practicing or trying (he's good at soccer and loves to talk about becoming a professional - and he may actually have the talent as confirmed by his coach at school - but he *certainly* doesn't have the necessary discipline to train properly).

It's really hard for me to deal with him because I was literally the opposite and all his weaknesses were my strengths and vice versa so I struggle with what to teach him - everything he struggles with came naturally to me and vice versa.

All of that is an introduction to saying that I have been doing discursive meditation based on your instructions on and off for a few months now and I have started to get him to "meditate" for the last week or so. That is, I make him sit down in one place without lying down or moving and just look at the wall for 10-15 mins.

He's slowly getting better at that, and I am thinking about telling him to imagine a soccer game in his head in detail during meditation (because he loves soccer).

Is there any other feasible way to get kids to do meditation? I am less interested in the symbolic magical stuff for him and more in just getting him to train his self-discipline, concentration, focus etc.

Any other ideas or suggestions or exercises for discipline and focus etc appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Kids meditation
replies.content:
According to Jung, children who enter a family often occupy the psychic space that the parents do NOT occupy already. Sort of like expanding into the power vacuum, or a soap bubble that can only take the shape of the empty space other bubbles are not in.

So you can go about this process of relating to your Other-ish kids is several ways.

1) You can spend the time and experience the awkwardness of developing the parts of your Totality that you are not good at -- -- becoming a more rounded and complete person. Your kid who has the native skills will likely try to help you grow up, so to speak into their world. By accepting their help, you open up the space for reciprocity and they may be more willing to accept your help in areas where they struggle. You also will create space for them to grow in areas that your already extant expertise is 'blocking' from their perspective.

2) You can meditate or pray and seek guidance to find out what set of skills your kid needs to develop in order to get a good solid start on a balanced and satisfying life. This will means sometimes playing to their strengths and encouraging them to Do The Work and reach far beyond what native gifts provide to help them achieve true excellence. Sometimes it will take the form of playing to their weaknesses and using their frustration and dismay to develop coping skills with negative emotions, avoiding punishing self-talk, learning 'tricks of the trade' that help you get by in an area you know you are weak in (like a finger-knuckle abacus for double-checking your poor mental arithmetic), and developing the calm self-acceptance that enables a person to be a good sport and appreciate other people's abilities. Deep breathing before tackling a challenging task or learning a new skill can be one such trick of the trade. What else do YOU use to create concentration and focus when you give a public talk or other task you are not naturally good at?

3) You can seek out tutors and masters in religious, spiritual, or martial art formats to help both you and your kids to strengthen the inner core that gives you self guidance and try not to worry as you develop these inward muscles of the soul.

4) You can teach yourself and your kid to use dreamwork, divination, artwork, to explore inner dimensions when the masters are not there to train and correct your beginning efforts.

5) You can spend time doing practical things together like regular chores, repairing things, making toys out of junk, building bird houses, go-carts, tool safety and clean-up, using a slide rule, etc. Likewise, shared physical things like fishing, hunting, bird watching, scouting, camping, map-making, and storytelling around the campfire. Physical attunement can bleed over into psychic attunement. Jon Young says that kids who are easily distracted make the best scouts because they are alert and aware to any changes or differences in the environment.

6) You can daily ask for help and guidance towards the best way to love those around you. This is the hardest path and the most sure.

No matter what mode of parenting you choose, it will take a lot of time and work and personal growth in your own soul. It requires nearly all the energy you have to create a healthy adult from the raw material of a child. You might need help from friends, relations and neighbors to achieve the best results. Ask for it.

THe same issues as elsewhere apply too - if he doesn't get something immediately he tends to give up easily even though he is talented (he was moved up to sparring with older heavier kids very quickly because he was just better than the other newbies).

So the martial arts is definitely good - he likes anything physical like that but he doesn't have the inherent passion for it that he does for soccer. He's not reading books (picture books) or watching endless Youtube videos about it like soccer.

But anyway I will meditate on and implement the suggestions given here - much appreciated, thank you.

I was more asking about how to adapt meditation for children - never expected such a broad variety of advice - this is great.

replies.title: Re: Kids meditation
replies.content:
Maybe my comment can be of some use:
I've been on the "other end" as a teacher, mostly of young men (military academies).
The most amazing breakthroughs, recognitions, moments of real and surprising insights happened just after I backed off.
Meaning I changed a paper from graded to pass/fail. (The sullen kid from Hawaii wrote that at this point, having seen what we threw at him, he did not feel ready to take on being an officer, the whole mess that goes with that.)
It was really intuitive. Genuine respect matters. Giving them room to experiment and fail, being honest, all those demonstrated, embodied qualities matter.

Meaning I gave the kids (students) a task, then faded into the background, simply listening, acknowledging. (Of course, those were rare times given the curriculum.)

Children, like most animals, imprint on someone. Maybe the
question isn't about your son. Your son will likely copy your behaviors in a few decades. Can you give yourself room to play, just a bit, without judgment, a bit of space to breathe?

I've found parent ego to be a consistent, perennial stumbling block (and, thus, useful for meditation).

comments.title: Cold Water
comments.content:
Not a question, but some data points for the ongoing discussion of cold showers:

1) Since the subject came up here, I've switched from just a quick cold rinse to keep my hair shiny, to showering in only cold water, most of the time (doesn't work for shaving, alas!). In just a few weeks, that went from clenching my teeth and making myself stay in for as long as it took to shout "THAT'S BRACING!" and recite an "Our Father", to gleefully heading in. The change to warm weather is a factor. As others have noted, feel like a million bucks getting out. Even in chilly weather, never felt shivery getting out. I recommend it!

2) Woke up one morning with a deep ache in one ankle, and the sole of that foot feeling unreasonably tender. Not the first time this has happened, and usually I suffer through it all morning. This time, I turned on the tap and ran cold water over it. And it was fine the rest of the day. Like it had never been sore. Tried that trick again after I pulled something in my leg while out digging the new garden bed... it helped, but it didn't magically make the pain go away. So: brilliant for some things. Not for everything.

3) Water (whether a shower, or floating in a lake), has always given me some relief from migraine. It's temporary, but there are no downsides (which you can't say for NSAIDs). Because I always have other things to get done, this is normally something I try *after* the pain is debilitating and I can't get anything else done anyway. Since I'm trying out cold water for everything now... the last couple of times a migraine came knocking, I doused myself in cold water as soon as the smallest niggling pain showed up. It did not prevent me from having a headache for the usual two or three days. But it DID prevent the pain from getting any worse. And that is MAGNIFICENT! I can function like a normal human being with a tiny niggling bit of head pain (and wet hair...). Sometimes the dousing needed repeating two or three times. The important part is that it worked.

--I have no idea why this works. Perhaps there is a perfectly ordinary explanation involving blood circulation. But I'm not a scientist and I won't be doing the research on it. This forum and our generous host have provided a useful lens for considering certain episodes of chronic-and-unreasonable pain: namely, that sometimes perhaps they are caused by etheric parasites and such critters don't like cold water.

Whether that's true or not is immaterial, so long as it works. You can tell me "improved circulation" all day long, and my imagination skims right over it, finding nothing to grab onto. Give me a mental image of I-hurt-because-invisible-leeches... and now suddenly I have an image that lets me take appropriate action. It works, and I'll take it. Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Cold Water
replies.content:
There's much more to this than just a cold shower. The cold shower is one type of Tattva Suddhi - element cleansing. In Tantric traditions every element can be done this way.

The mud baths and masques at spas is tattva suddhi for the earth element. At certain wind speeds if you stand in the wind it will likewise cleanse the air element.

Most interesting to me is what Isha Center discovered when they revived the Tantric Fire element cleansing ritual. If you move a flame oil lamp in a certain pattern around a person's aura it will be as invigorating as the cold shower. They also discovered that (unlike the cold shower) it will cure children with ADHD after a few weeks of regular fire tattva suddhi treatments. The kids never need meds or any other medical treatment ever again for that particular problem.

1) As in, a Cthulhu Cthursday or something like that? I'll consider it. I'm feeling a little bereft right now -- a couple of days ago I finished writing the last novel I intend to write in the Haliverse, The Seal of Yuen Lao, and that meant saying goodbye to a people who have been constant presences in my inner life for six years now; the mere fact that they're imaginary doesn't make the parting any less of a wrench.

2) My background in conjure is somewhat limited -- I studied with Cat Yronwode and use her students-only textbook as my main guide, though Carolyn Morrow Long's book Spiritual Merchants is a good history. As for blues tracks, I'm not actually much of a blues fan -- that's something I put into Innsmouth to add verisimilitude and flavor.

3) Most writers these days come from the leftward end of the political spectrum and, for reasons I'm still not sure I understand, way too many people on that end of things have a weird set of mental blocks about guns. More generally, though, howlers like that are excruciatingly common in historical fiction of all kinds. It's rare for me to read a novel set in a different time period that doesn't have people using out-of-period slang, say, or behaving in ways that nobody behaved in that time and place.

4) I have no idea. We'll just have to see if anybody's prepared to act.

replies.title: Re: Bunch-O-Stuff
replies.content:
I'm not sure about "weird" regarding the Left's mental block on guns; I've mulled over the issue before and I suspect there's at least 3-4 reasons for it (some good, some not):

1) There is at least an implicit belief among large parts of the more activist Left that in practice the Second Amendment is mere words on paper as far as they're concerned - and, moreover, that this *only* applies to them and not to right-wing activists. (I'm frankly not sure they're wrong about that, for a half-dozen reasons, some of which are historically contingent - I suspect "showing guns is an excuse for the authorities to kill you, and claiming self-defense gets you the death penalty if you're lucky" got handed down to varying degrees from least three antecedents of the modern left [the labor movement, LGBT, and the broader civil rights movement] - and and others ("the authorities are, for obvious reasons, more charitably inclined towards paramilitaries acting to support the status quo") less so. In fact, I'm pretty sure the bailey version of that belief is right: it's commonplace in those parts that left-wing protestors simply would not be allowed to get away with everyone bringing a gun to a protest the way right-wing militias are, and I don't think they're wrong about that.) That's not the only key driver of gun control support on the left (class issues are also involved), but it's one of them - key parts of the left believe a) that they are de facto not allowed to have guns and their opponents *are* and b) past evidence (notably the wave of gun control legislation passed in reaction to the Black Panthers in the 1970s) indicates that realizing their de jure right to bear arms themselves is probably beyond their capabilities - but the other form of equalizing (removing the right to bear arms from their opponents) might actually be achievable.
2) The successes of the Civil Rights movement and a couple of decolonization movements (at least Gandhi) got fused to the myth of progress, leading to a belief that we'd found a better way of social change than bloody revolution. (I very much suspect at least some of this is mythologizing a virtue out of perceived necessity deriving from point 1.)
3) I really wouldn't be shocked if part of both of the above was explicit psy-ops from programs like COINTELPRO, above and beyond any basic intimidation tactics. (This may also effect writing about guns directly - there's been some declassifications the last few years indicating that one of the CIA's Cold War activities was funding mid-century American art, notably Jackson Pollack (to quote someone else's take, "when we said freedom of expression we meant it"); given other intelligence agency actions of the era, the possibility of one or more three-letter agencies funding lefty writers with a distaste for guns sounds quite plausible to me.)
4) For a bunch of reasons (especially in the US where the white ethnics came into it), the twentieth-century left tended to be an urban phenomenon, and weapons being more strongly regulated in cities was an old commonplace even before guns (with their "have you looked at what you're using as a backstop?" issue) became common, probably due to population density and cumulative probabilities of low-odds events.

- pretentious_username

comments.title: What is the will?
comments.content:
Dear Grand Archdruid (Emeritus),

What is the magical definition of will and what is the best way to train it? Would you have a book to recommend on this topic?

As a second question, what originally convinced you of the existence of reincarnation? What would be the evidence in support?

replies.title: Re: What is the will?
replies.content:
1) The will is the aspect of the self that enables you to choose. The best way to train it is to do things for no reason other than to train the will. What kind of things? Anything, so long as it has no reward in and of itself. Set your alarm five minutes early and leave it there even though you don't need the extra time. Make a point of noticing every red thing in the room around you. Decide to be at a certain place you've never visited before at exactly 1:34 pm next Saturday and keep the appointment to the minute. And, of course, set yourself a schedule of magical practices and maintain them no matter what. I don't have a book to recommend; there used to be dozens of them in Victorian times, but they're all long out of print and I doubt you can find them.

2) Remembering my own past lives had quite a bit to do with that. You might find Ian Stevenson's volumes of research into past life memories worth reading as evidence.

replies.title: Re: Gurdjieff
replies.content:
I looked into Gurdjieff and the Fourth Way briefly during my first stint in college, inspired by a good film version of Meetings with Remarkable Men, and decided that it wasn't for me -- partly the teachings didn't have anything like the appeal of the Golden Dawn system, partly everybody I met at that time who was into the Fourth Way had that humorless dogmatic sort of personality one associates with fanatics. (I steered clear of Rudolf Steiner's Anthroposophy for exactly the same reasons.) Over the years that followed I got a clear sense of just how much there was to master in the occult traditions I'd chosen to study, and so I never looked back.

I tend to class Gurdjieff in the same category as Steiner, Meher Baba, Sri Aurobindo, et al. -- the early 20th century had plenty of figures like that, each equipped with a idiosyncratic system of spiritual development, each surrounded by a cult of personality. I'll probably get to reading the works of at least some of them eventually, but it's not at the top of my list.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings Archdruid,

During antiquity and early Medieval age, Ireland was more isolated from the cultural influence of Rome and of Catholic Church than Britain. So, there was not a significant armed or clerical force to wipe out Druidry from Ireland. However, still, Druids in Ireland somehow vanished suddenly. I find this very baffling. What is the reason of this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In her book The Rise of Magic in Early Medieval Europe, Valerie Flint makes a case that this happened across much of Europe for an unexpected reason: in those days, Christian clerics were better at magic than their Pagan opponents. I think she's likely to be correct. Christianity in its pre-Reformation era was full of overtly magical practices, and those drew on the energies set in motion by the Piscean age; most of the older Pagan traditions were still using the formulae of the departed Arian age. (That wasn't true of Norse Heathenry, interestingly enough -- Odin is a Piscean deity, as you can tell by his association with self-sacrifice -- which may explain why Heathenry put up so much more vigorous a fight against Christianity than most other European religions.)

Now, of course, the shoe is on the other foot: Western Christianity discarded most of its magical practices at the time of the Reformation and ditched the remainder in the late 20th century in a misguided attempt to be "relevant." Christianity has now become a minority faith in its historic heartlands in Europe and the Middle East, and it's rapidly approaching minority-faith status in the US; meanwhile a lively religious ferment is under way in those regions, out of which the faiths of the Aquarian age, equipped with robust magic in their turn, will doubtless emerge.

Druidry might end up providing some of the raw material, or not, but a new religious movement comes out of the white-hot incandescence of spiritual experience, not out of a quirky, bookish, deliberately eccentric intellectual faith like mine.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure. The basic themes of the Aquarian age are freedom, eccentricity, innovation, and isolation; Uranus, its planetary ruler, is the planet of the individual, and its discovery heralded the rise of republican governments and the wider spread of the concept of individual rights.  Viðarr, the son and avenger of Odin, is a classic Aquarian deity; craftsman-gods who come into being in solitude and silence, such as Ptah, are Aquarian; a case could be made that Pan, who dwells alone in the wilderness and brings terror ("panic") with his shout, is an Aquarian god. On the other hand, the Roman astrologer Manilius assigns Hera to Aquarius.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The people who taught you that there's no Christian occultism were either misinformed or lying. The vast majority of Western occultism between 600 CE and 1800 CE was explicitly Christian. Many of the classic grimoires assume that you're a practicing Catholic and will go to Mass and confess your sins before you conjure spirits in the name of Jesus, for heaven's sake! Most European folk magic is robustly Christian, and casts spells and consecrates amulets in the name of Jesus and the saints. If you're interested in high magic, most of the branches of the Rosicrucian movement are explicitly Christian; so is Anthroposophy, Rudolf Steiner's offshoot of the Theosophical movement; so is the Martinist Order, one of the major systems of magical lodge work in the world today. If you'd like a taste of what Christian occultism is like, pick up a copy of Gareth Knight's book Experience of the Inner Worlds sometime -- it's a training manual of Christian Cabalistic magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The symbols of Air and Earth in the AODA/Universal Gnostic tradition are idiosyncratic. I was never given an explanation of why they're used in place of some other symbol, but they make great fodder for meditation, and they also work very well in practice.

2) Of all the Asian mystical philosophies, Taoism is probably the closest to the Druid Revival tradition; I know Druids who consider the Tao Te Ching to be among their holy books, for example. I know of no reason why the I Ching should be inappropriate.

3) If you don't feel comfortable working with deities, don't work with deities. Put more effort and concentration into your SoP workings to make up for their absence.

4) It seems to me that you've sketched out the standard effects very precisely here. Regular use of a protective ritual such as the SoP, and close attention to grounding yourself in material reality, should help.

comments.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Partly it's from the media, which makes a specialty of getting people terrified of things they can't do anything about. It's part of the basic mechanism of advertising -- you feed people all kinds of upsetting stuff, and then show them ads that are all full of happy smiling people and no problems at all, or problems that are quickly and easily solved. That prompts people to run mindlessly out and buy consumer crap in a reflexive attempt to be like the people in the ads.

Still, there's another factor. If you're wasting your life, the approach of death is terrifying, because it reminds you that you only have so many days to live and if you waste them they're gone. Since so many people waste their lives these days, I suspect that plays a very large role.

comments.title: Another Magic Monday!
comments.content:
I was recently reminded of a passage in a book which seems attributed to William Walker Atkinson, ‘The Inner Secet: That Something Within, by X’. Have you have read this neat and obscure little book? If so, I am curious how it struck you, and what you may think he meant?

Page 147 in my copy (or 91 in an online pdf I copied from today) have the passage which stuck my memory recently. I’m posting it here, if that is okay.

(Or in a comment below, if that is a better place, I am not much familiar with posting yet)


"I progressed rapidly, rising from high places to those still higher. I became
known nationally—then internationally—as a “great captain of industry”, and as one who had “arrived” and had “done things”. I became one of those who combined and concentrated separate industries into what were called
“trusts”. I became a great organizer—a builder of industries—a, creator of organizations. I have been lauded as a world benefactor, and I have been abused as an enemy of the race—all depending upon the point of view of those making the criticism. Personally, I do not regard either of these descriptions as correct; I think rather that I was an impersonal dynamic force building and creating things and conditions which, while serving a useful purpose and service in the evolution of things, will eventually be torn down and their foundations employed as the base of far better things and conditions.
I neither seek credit, nor do I fear adverse criticism, for what I have done. In truth, I sought neither to advance the freedom of mankind nor to bind it in chains."

He then elaborates more in an occult context, and I am also curious for your conjecture as well:

"I neither seek credit, nor do I fear adverse criticism, for what I have done. In truth, I sought neither to advance the freedom of mankind nor to bind it in chains. I thought but little about such things, in fact. I have tried to cause as little distress as possible; I have endeavored to help individual men, and the race in general, as much as I could—this simply because I “felt that way”, and not from a sense that such was my mission.

My “mission” has always seemed to me to be that of manifesting the Creative Energy of which I am a focal point or centre of expression. I built, I created, I put-together—sometimes I was compelled to tear down in order to build better things on the old foundations. Men have sometimes called me ruthless—I may have been so, but, if so, only because the Creative Energy of that Something Within insisted that its work was to be done, no matter what stood in its way. Most positively, I have never maliciously or revengefully worked harm to any one. I have been as impersonal and as elemental as a natural force, both in my constructive and my destructive activities. But, while I mastered—I also served. The Law of Balanced Compensation decrees that “The Master must render Service.” Service is the “other side” of the shield of Mastery.

Men have accused me of being avaricious, greedy, mercenary—a selfish, a rapacious money-grabber—and, indeed, I do not doubt but that I appeared as such to them, for they could see only the outside of things and not the inner motives. In truth, I have met but few men to whom money as money, or possessions as possessions, were of as little consequence as to myself. Beyond the comparatively small amount of money required to keep me in comfort—to furnish me with reasonable necessities with a moderate admixture of luxuries—money and possessions have meant nothing to me. Money I have regarded as but the counters of the great game which I have played so successfully—the game itself was the real thing to me. I was like the boy who earnestly plays the game for the sake of the game—not for the cheap marbles which are the apparent objects for which he plays."

I look forward to each Magic Monday as there’s no page like it I have seen; it’s really great to see so many active and diverse minds on one page!

Many thanks JMG and readers

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday!
replies.content: I'm not at all familiar with the book, and that doesn't sound like Atkinson -- but it's very interesting stuff to meditate on.

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday!
replies.content: It's actully by a long-term associate of Atkinson's, Edward E. Beals.

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday!
replies.content: I couldn't find any biographical detail about Edward E. Beals. Was he a real person, or another pseudonym used by Atkinson?

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday!
replies.content:
I think Edward E. Beals was a real person. I found him, IIRC, in one or two of the US census returns for Chicago the early 20th century. Atkinson may have done some editing on Beals' books before they were published, but the styles of the two men seem quite different to me. Beals and Atkinson seem to have been business partners of long standing in publishing New Thought books.

Not all of the pseudonymous works published by Atkinson's major publishing outlets were written by Atkinson. "Swami Bhakti Vishita" and "Swami Panchadasi" are almost certainly pseudonyms of the esotericist, stage conjuror and con-artist Claude Alexander Conlin (aka "Alexander the Great, the Man Who Knows"), according to people who knew the man well in his later years.* See the biography of Conlin by David Charvet. "Magus Incognito" is Clifford Edgar Brooksmith (or Brocksmith). Atkinson and Conlin seem to go way back; the relationship between Atkinson and Brooksmith is less clear to me.

"Yogi Ramacharaka," "Theron Q. Dumont" and "Theodore Sheldon" are definitely Atkinson, IMHO, to judge by the style and the content.
_____
* Successful con-artists, sincere religious or spiritual counselors and skilled diviners have one thing in common: to be really good at what they do, they have to be really skilled at "reading" people, at knowing things about them that the people themselves can not bear to bring up to conscious awareness. It is not unknown for one and the same person to function in all three capacities, as circumstances mandate. Since everyone is some sort of difficult mix of morality and immorality, this troubling fact ought not to be a surprise. Conlin was very clearly all three rolled up together in one body. --= PS It's not an inborn skill, not to the extent that I'm speaking of here. One has to train oneself long and hard, or be trained by difficult circumnstances, to develop it to any high degree.

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday!

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday!
replies.content:
Elias Gewurtz was a well-documented real person, a Jewish member of the Theosophical Society at London who later relocated to the Theosophical community at Crotona (in California), and who wrote books for other publishing houses in addeition to Atkinson's ventures. See, for example, Boaz Huss, " 'Qabbala: The Theos-Sophia of the Jews': Jewish Theosophists and Their Perception of Kabbalah," freely available online at academia.edu (You have to log on to the websiter to read papers there, but anyone can do that for free by way of either facebook or google/gmail.)

BTW, Boaz Huss seems also to have uncovered interesting new things about the H. B. of L, Max Theon and his wife. I haven't read those papers yet, but they look promising.

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday!
replies.content:
There is an important quote in the "Publisher's Statement" part of that book:

“If it suits better the purposes of publication to announce the story under
the classification of fiction, by all means do so—but here I make the distinction
between the terms, “fiction” and “fictitious”, respectively. In the form and
arrangement of its presentation, this story may be technically classified as
fiction—but in its essential substance it is fact, actuality, truth. Again, some
portions of the story may be regarded as allegorical rather than as literal—
but, as the transcendentalists ever tell us, there are certain high truths, and
certain deep human experiences, which are capable of expression only
through symbols and the forms of metaphor and allegory; many an allegory
is the expression of actual human experience in the form of symbols. Once
more, I suggest that you let the story speak for itself. Those who recognize
Truth in whatever form it may present itself will know! just how true is this
story; those who do not know Truth even when it is in plain sight—who fail
to see the forest because of the presence of the trees—they will see naught
but mere fiction in this narrative. This is as it should be—it does not matter
in the least.”
